nodes.title: chore(deps): bump qs from 6.15.0 to 6.15.2 in /mcp-server

nodes.body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Correct inflated estimated cost in incident proof**

The incident artifact reports `Estimated cost: $0.1020`, but the accompanying trace file only contains three billable `cost_usd` events (0.0135, 0.017, 0.0205), which sum to `$0.0510`. This doubles the demonstrated spend for the same run and can mislead anyone using this proof bundle to validate cost accounting or tune budget thresholds, so the generated report value should be corrected or the trace/report pair regenerated to stay internally consistent.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

The proof bundle hard-codes an absolute local checkout path (including a real Windows username). For a public repo proof artifact this is unnecessarily identifying and reduces reproducibility; consider redacting to `C:\Users\<USER>\...` (or using a placeholder like `<WORKTREE>`), while keeping the repo-bound import point intact.

This issue also appears on line 10 of the same file.

The import-binding output includes an absolute local path with a real username. Please redact to a placeholder path (e.g., `C:\Users\<USER>\...`) to avoid publishing personal workstation details while still demonstrating that the import is repo-bound.

The `guard.budget_exceeded` event stores the *cumulative* spend under `data.cost_usd` (0.051). `summarize_trace()` treats any `cost_usd` (top-level or `data.cost_usd`) as an additive cost line item, so this key causes the incident/report cost to be double-counted. To match `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl` (which uses `data.cost_used`) and keep reports accurate, rename this field to `cost_used` (or remove it) and regenerate the derived incident/report artifacts.

The incident report shows `Estimated cost: $0.1020`, but the review iterations total $0.0510 and the trace’s `guard.budget_exceeded` reason is also `$0.0510 > $0.0450`. This mismatch comes from the trace double-counting cumulative cost (via `guard.budget_exceeded.data.cost_usd`). After fixing the trace field (e.g., rename to `cost_used`), regenerate this incident report so the displayed cost matches the actual spend being evidenced.

The incident reporter only surfaces guard-event details from `data.message` (see `sdk/agentguard/reporting.py:_event_message`). This example emits `guard.budget_exceeded` with `data.reason`, so the generated incident report drops the explanatory message. Prefer `data.message` (and keep `reason` only if you intentionally want a separate field).

The `agentguard report` / `agentguard incident` commands reference `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl` and `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`, but the recorded artifacts in this proof are suffixed with `_run9` (and the “Guard Events Observed” section also uses the `_run9` filenames). Align the command examples with the actual artifact filenames, or add an explicit note that the files were renamed when copied into `proof/dogfood/2026-05-22/`.

The lines marking `ops/03-ROADMAP_NOW_NEXT_LATER.md` and `ops/02-ARCHITECTURE.md` as “stale by repo policy” assert a policy that isn’t referenced here. Consider either (a) linking/citing the specific freshness policy location, or (b) rephrasing to just state the observed “Last reviewed” age without claiming it violates a policy.

This file starts with a UTF-8 BOM (rendered as a leading `﻿` before the first character). BOMs can show up as stray characters in diffs/rendering and can break exact text matching in scripts. Consider saving this as UTF-8 *without* BOM so the first character is `#`.
This report begins with a UTF-8 BOM (rendered as a leading `﻿`). Consider saving as UTF-8 without BOM to avoid the invisible leading character affecting downstream parsing/grepping.

nodes.title: fix: report source version in local doctor runs

nodes.body: _read_source_version() trusts any ../pyproject.toml and grabs the first `version = "..."` line anywhere in that file. If `agentguard` is imported from an environment where the parent directory happens to contain an unrelated pyproject.toml (or multiple version keys), `__version__` can be wrong. Consider constraining the match to the `[project]` table and/or validating `name = "agentguard47"` before accepting the version; otherwise fall back to importlib.metadata.

Constrained `_read_source_version()` to the `[project]` table and now require `name = agentguard47` before accepting the source version. Added focused tests for both the non-AgentGuard parent case and the wrong-section version case. Validation: `python -m pytest sdk/tests/test_init.py -q` and `python -m pytest sdk/tests/test_doctor.py sdk/tests/test_demo.py -q`, plus repo-local `python -m agentguard.cli doctor` with `PYTHONPATH=./sdk`.

nodes.title: Add audit-only MCP tool-call audit-log primitive

nodes.body:
## CodeQL / Use of a broken or weak cryptographic hashing algorithm on sensitive data

[Sensitive data (secret)](1) is used in a hashing algorithm (SHA1) that is insecure.
[Sensitive data (secret)](2) is used in a hashing algorithm (SHA1) that is insecure.

[Show more details](https://github.com/bmdhodl/agent47/security/code-scanning/54)

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Accept path-like sinks in MCPAuditLogger**

`MCPAuditLogger` currently only treats `str` as a file path, so passing a common `pathlib.Path` (for example from `tmp_path` fixtures) raises `TypeError` even though `JsonlFileSink` and `open()` can handle path-like objects. This makes the new API unexpectedly stricter than the rest of the SDK’s sink/path handling and will break normal usage unless callers manually cast with `str(...)`.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Enforce cost budget using actual per-call cost**

The loop always calls `budget.consume(..., cost_usd=0.0)`, so `max_cost_usd` can never trigger even in `live` mode. This breaks the documented behavior when users point the template at a paid OpenAI-compatible endpoint (the run can exceed the configured dollar cap while appearing guarded). Use `estimate_cost(...)` (or provider-reported cost) per call before consuming the budget.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Guard against missing tool path before file read**

If the model emits `TOOL_CALL: read_file` with malformed or missing JSON args, `parse_tool_call` falls back to `{}` and this line passes `""` as the path, which resolves to `base_dir` and raises `IsADirectoryError`. Because only `BudgetExceeded` is caught, the example crashes instead of logging/handling a bad tool call. Validate that `path` is a non-empty file path and treat invalid args as a denied/failed tool call.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

`parse_tool_call` uses the PEP 604 union operator (`Dict[str, Any] | None`), which is invalid syntax on Python 3.9. CI runs tests on 3.9, so this example will fail to even import/compile there. Use `Optional[Dict[str, Any]]` (or `typing.Optional`) instead.

In live mode, token accounting can silently become a no-op: if the local server omits the `usage` field (or its token keys), `_live_reply` returns 0 tokens and the loop consumes `tokens=0`, so `BudgetGuard(max_tokens=...)` won’t actually cap tokens. Consider falling back to a rough token estimate when usage is missing (or emitting a warning + consuming an estimate) so the “budget cap” claim holds across common local servers.

`RateLimitGuard.check()` raises `BudgetExceeded` (per sdk/agentguard/guards.py), but the handler prints it as `[budget STOP] ...`, which is misleading when the stop reason is rate limiting. Catch rate-limit failures separately (or print a neutral label like `[guard STOP]`) so the output matches the actual guard that fired.

If the model emits a malformed tool call (invalid JSON or missing `path`), `parse_tool_call` returns `{}` for args and the loop calls `read_file_tool` with an empty path. That resolves to `base_dir` and `read_text()` will raise (e.g., `IsADirectoryError`), crashing the template in live mode. Treat malformed/missing tool args as a denied tool call (and log it) instead of defaulting to `{}`.

`AgentPolicy` is marked `frozen=True` but `allowed_tools` is a mutable `List[str]`, so callers can still mutate the allowlist in-place (`policy.allowed_tools.append(...)`) despite the object being “frozen”. If immutability is intended here, prefer an immutable type (e.g. `Tuple[str, ...]`) or expose it as a read-only sequence.

nodes.title: Add custom resource attributes and span links to OtelTraceSink

nodes.body:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Guard link attribute parsing against non-dict values**

When a span-start event includes a `links` entry whose `attributes` field is not a mapping (for example a string or list), `_build_links` calls `.items()` unconditionally and raises `AttributeError`, which aborts `emit()` for that span. This is a runtime regression introduced with link support, and it contradicts the intended behavior of skipping malformed link entries; the sink should validate `attributes` is a dict (or fall back to `{}`) before iterating.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

Good catch. Fixed in 998fe47 — `_build_links` now coerces a non-dict `attributes` field to `{}` before iterating, and a regression test (`test_link_with_non_dict_attributes_does_not_crash`) covers it.

Malformed `links` values are not fully guarded here: if `links` is present but not iterable (for example `True` or `1`), this loop raises `TypeError` and the span start fails instead of skipping the malformed input as the new API contract describes. Validate that `raw_links` is a list/tuple (or other intended iterable) before iterating.
Fixed in 20bf459. `_build_links` now returns early when `links` is truthy but not a list/tuple, so malformed scalar values are skipped instead of raising during span start.

This still crashes for malformed link attributes such as `{"span_id": "source", "attributes": "bad"}` because non-empty non-dict values do not have `.items()`. Since the new behavior promises malformed link entries are skipped without crashing, guard `attributes` with an `isinstance(..., dict)` check before building `link_attrs`.
Fixed in 20bf459. Non-dict `attributes` values are now coerced to `{}` before iteration, and the regression test coverage remains in `test_link_with_non_dict_attributes_does_not_crash`.

The referenced line number is stale: `self._spans` is at `sdk/agentguard/sinks/otel.py:80` in this diff, not `otel.py:70`. Keeping evidence artifacts accurate matters because future reviewers may use these citations to verify the implementation.

This issue also appears on line 12 of the same file.
Updated in 20bf459. The evidence note now points at the current `self._spans` location in `otel.py`.

This malformed-input test only covers bad list entries and missing `span_id`; it does not cover a non-list `links` value or a link whose `attributes` value is non-dict. Those cases are part of the new malformed-input contract and should be covered to prevent regressions in the link parsing path.
Addressed in 20bf459. Added `test_non_iterable_links_value_skipped` alongside the existing malformed-attributes coverage so both malformed-link cases are pinned.

This PASS statement is too broad: the implementation only handles non-dict entries, missing `span_id`, unknown span IDs, and falsey attributes. Non-list `links` values and non-dict truthy `attributes` values still raise exceptions, so the QA evidence should not claim malformed input is fully defended until those cases are fixed/tested.

This issue also appears on line 26 of the same file.
Updated in 20bf459. QA now states the narrower, accurate malformed-input contract: non-list `links`, non-dict entries, missing span IDs, unknown span IDs, and non-dict attrs are skipped safely.

This completion checkbox is not satisfied yet: malformed link inputs are only partially handled, and cases like a non-iterable `links` value or non-dict `attributes` value can still raise during span start. Leave this unchecked or broaden the validation/tests before marking it complete.
Updated in 20bf459. The work-plan completion note now explicitly includes truthy non-list `links` values and non-dict link attributes.

nodes.title: Make coding-agent review loop proof deterministic

nodes.body: The repo binding/commands include absolute Windows paths with a local username (e.g., `C:\Users\patri\...`). This leaks local machine/user info and makes the proof less portable. Consider replacing with repo-relative paths (e.g., `<repo_root>\sdk`) or a placeholder like `C:\<user>\...`.

The "Commands Run" section references `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl` and `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`, but this proof bundle only includes `agentguard-demo-traces-run7.jsonl` and `coding-agent-review-loop-traces-run7.jsonl`. This makes the bundle non-replayable as written; either include copies with the referenced filenames (or update the commands/notes to point at the committed artifact paths).

This output says the trace was written to `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl`, but the committed artifact is `agentguard-demo-traces-run7.jsonl`. If the intent is a replayable proof bundle, consider also committing a copy under the original filename (or note/reflect the rename so readers can follow the commands without guessing).

This output references `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`, but the bundle contains `coding-agent-review-loop-traces-run7.jsonl`. For replayability, either add a copy with the referenced filename or clarify that the trace was moved/renamed when bundling the proof.

`guard.budget_exceeded` includes `data.cost_usd` as 0.051 (the running total), which causes reporting to count that value as additional cost on top of the per-iteration `cost_usd` events (yielding the $0.1020 incident estimate). To keep cost accounting consistent with SDK trace semantics, this field should represent the incremental cost of the call that exceeded (or be renamed to something like `cost_used`/`total_cost_usd`).

The incident report's estimated cost ($0.1020) appears to double-count relative to the three review iteration costs totaling $0.0510, because the trace also includes `guard.budget_exceeded` with `data.cost_usd=0.051`. Either regenerate the trace with consistent cost fields (incremental per-call cost on guard events) or add a short note explaining the accounting so readers don't assume the report is wrong.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Replace Windows separators in documented run commands**

Use `/` instead of `\` in these shell commands. In bash/zsh (the repo’s documented environment), backslash escapes the next character, so `python examples\coding_agent_review_loop.py` is interpreted as `python examplescoding_agent_review_loop.py` and fails. This makes the proof steps non-reproducible for Linux/macOS users copying the runbook.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

The trace filenames referenced in the command list (`agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl`, `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`) don’t match the trace files committed in this folder (which are suffixed `*-run6.jsonl`). Please make the naming consistent (either rename the committed trace files to the unsuffixed names, since the run folder already namespaces them, or update the commands/artifact references to match the actual filenames).

This issue also appears in the following locations of the same file:
- line 59
- line 62

The incident report’s “Estimated cost: $0.1020” doesn’t match the costs present in the committed `coding_agent_review_loop_traces-run6.jsonl` (0.0135 + 0.0170 + 0.0205 = $0.0510). Please regenerate this report from the committed trace file, or add a short note explaining why the estimate differs so readers don’t interpret it as a math error.

This `trace` filename (`agentguard_doctor_trace.jsonl`) doesn’t match the committed artifact name in this folder (`agentguard_doctor_trace-run6.jsonl`). Please align so this index points to the actual file.

This issue also appears in the following locations of the same file:
- line 27
- line 69

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Use POSIX path separator in proof command**

This recorded command is not runnable in the repository’s documented shell environments (bash/zsh): `python examples\coding_agent_review_loop.py` treats `\c` as an escape and resolves to `examplescoding_agent_review_loop.py`, so reproducibility of the run log breaks for Unix-like users even though the file claims `EXIT: 0`. Use `python examples/coding_agent_review_loop.py` (or an OS-agnostic form) so the proof can be replayed reliably.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

The command list/report inputs reference unsuffixed trace filenames (e.g., agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl, coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl), but the committed raw artifacts are suffixed with `-run5` (e.g., agentguard_demo_traces-run5.jsonl). This makes it hard to reproduce the reported `agentguard report/incident` outputs from the checked-in files. Consider either (a) committing the traces under the exact filenames used during the run, or (b) updating the commands and any referenced filenames in this summary to match the `-run5` artifacts (or explicitly noting the rename).

The incident report's "Estimated cost: $0.1020" conflicts with the budget-exceeded details in the same run (budget exceeded at $0.0510 > $0.0450). With the current checked-in trace, this appears to be double-counting (per-iteration costs plus the cumulative total embedded in the `guard.budget_exceeded` event). Please regenerate this incident report from a trace that doesn't include cumulative cost as an additional `cost_usd` contribution (or adjust the trace schema/fields so reports compute the intended total).

This `guard.budget_exceeded` event includes `data.cost_usd` as the cumulative budget total (0.051), while earlier `review.iteration` events already emit per-call `cost_usd`. The CLI report/incident summarizers sum all extractable `cost_usd` values, so this makes the overall estimated cost come out to 0.102 instead of the intended 0.051. Consider removing `cost_usd` from this guard event (or renaming it to something like `cost_used`/`total_cost_usd` that reporters don't treat as additive) and then re-running the example to regenerate the committed trace + reports.

This proof artifact includes an absolute local filesystem path with a Windows username (e.g., `C:\Users\patri\...`). Since this repo is public, please redact personal machine paths (use a repo-relative path like `sdk/agentguard/__init__.py`, or replace the username segment with a placeholder) to avoid leaking PII and to keep proofs portable.

Fixed in b6ece29 by replacing the absolute Windows path in repo-import.txt with the repo-relative path sdk/agentguard/__init__.py. Re-ran the focused pytest slice, release guard, and git diff --check after the change.

This file is named `.json` but it is not valid JSON: it contains two top-level JSON objects separated by unquoted filename lines. If anything ever tries to parse this as JSON (or validate it), it will fail. Consider making it a single JSON object keyed by trace filename (or an array of `{trace_file, counts, details}` objects), or rename the file to `.txt` if the mixed format is intentional.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Store guard event summary as valid JSON**

This artifact is named `.json` but starts with a bare filename token instead of a JSON value, so standard parsers fail immediately (`JSONDecodeError` at line 1). Any automation that ingests dogfood proof artifacts as JSON (for validation, dashboards, or regression checks) will break on this file; wrap both trace summaries in a single JSON object/array or split them into separate `.json` files.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

The file starts with a UTF-8 BOM (rendered as an invisible "\ufeff" before the first '#'). This makes diffs noisier and can trip tooling that expects plain UTF-8 without BOM.

This issue also appears on line 10 of the same file.
This report file starts with a UTF-8 BOM (invisible "\ufeff" before the first character). Prefer plain UTF-8 without BOM to avoid noisy diffs and parser edge cases.
This incident report starts with a UTF-8 BOM (invisible "\ufeff" before the first '#'). Prefer plain UTF-8 without BOM to avoid noisy diffs and parser edge cases.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Correct no-network claim in proof scope**

The scope line says no network was used, but the same proof run includes `python scripts\sdk_release_guard.py --check-mcp-npm`, and that flag is explicitly a networked npm-registry check (`scripts/sdk_release_guard.py` describes it as “Uses network”). This makes the artifact internally inconsistent and can mislead readers auditing whether the run was truly offline.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

The scope says “No network/API keys… none used by the proof commands”, but the command list includes `scripts\sdk_release_guard.py --check-mcp-npm`, which performs npm registry checks (network). Please either remove the npm check from the “no network” proof run or explicitly call out that this step uses network while still requiring no API keys.

The commands reference `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl` / `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`, but the committed artifacts in this folder are suffixed `*-run4.jsonl`. This makes the reproduction steps confusing; please make the filenames consistent (either update the commands and “Trace written to …” references, or rename the artifacts to match the generated filenames and rely on the directory name for disambiguation).

This output says the trace was written to `agentguard_demo_traces.jsonl` and suggests report/incident commands using that filename, but the trace artifact committed in this folder is `agentguard_demo_traces-run4.jsonl`. Please align the output capture with the committed filenames (or include an explicit note that the file was renamed for archival).

This output says the trace was written to `coding_agent_review_loop_traces.jsonl`, but the committed trace file is `coding_agent_review_loop_traces-run4.jsonl`. Please align the referenced filename with the committed artifact (or note that it was renamed).

The doctor output indicates it wrote `agentguard_doctor_trace.jsonl`, but the committed artifact is `agentguard_doctor_trace-run4.jsonl`. Please keep filenames consistent across the captured output and committed artifacts (or document any post-run renaming).

The doctor output indicates it wrote `agentguard_doctor_trace.jsonl`, but the committed artifact is `agentguard_doctor_trace-run4.jsonl`. Please keep filenames consistent across the captured output and committed artifacts (or document any post-run renaming).

The proof text includes an absolute local Windows path with a specific username (`C:\Users\patri\...`). This is personal/workstation-specific information and makes the proof less reusable; consider redacting the username/path (e.g., `C:\Users\<user>\...`) or describing the repo location generically.

This issue also appears on line 11 of the same file.

The claim that the review loop stopped "without ... network calls" isn’t clearly supported by the included artifacts (e.g., the demo trace contains `tool.error` entries described as "upstream timeout"). Consider tightening the wording to avoid asserting "no network calls" unless the traces/proof explicitly demonstrate that those tool failures are simulated/offline.

nodes.title: chore(deps): bump ruff from 0.15.12 to 0.15.13 in /.github/requirements
chore(deps): bump fast-uri from 3.1.0 to 3.1.2 in /mcp-server
